narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st decile, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical 6th decile average among those who passed the bar. This low ranking signaled to the model a potential challenge in sustaining strong performance through later years, strongly tipping the scales against you. Next in influence, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 worked in your favor, as it was close to the 3.24 average for bar passers and pulled the prediction somewhat toward success. Your LSAT score of 25.5 had a milder but negative effect, falling well below the 37 average for successful students and nudging the odds toward failure. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket—which aligns with about a third of bar passers—and your plan to attend full-time, which is common among successful students, played smaller roles overall. Compared to typical admitted applicants, who tend to show higher decile rankings in later school years and stronger entrance exam scores, your profile stood out as riskier in those key areas despite some solid elements like your undergrad record. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort, but a data-driven flag based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the mid-30s range seen in passers, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, or exploring programs with bridge courses or bar prep support that could address these predictors. You might also gather letters highlighting your resilience or non-academic strengths to supplement future applications. Many students in similar positions have improved their profiles and succeeded—persistence pays off here. Keep building on your undergrad foundation, as that already gives you a positive edge.
